questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A body of mass $M$ is placed on a rough horizontal surface having a friction coefficient $\mu$. A person attempts to pull the body by applying a horizontal force, yet the body remains stationary. If the force exerted by the surface on the body is $F$, then
questions.solution:
The force exerted by the surface on the body is the resultant contact force, which is the vector sum of the normal reaction $N$ and the force of static friction $f$.

$F = \sqrt{N^2 + f^2}$

Since the body is in vertical equilibrium on a horizontal surface, the normal reaction is equal to the weight of the body.

$N = Mg$

The body remains stationary under the applied horizontal force, so the static friction $f$ is self-adjusting. Its value ranges from zero to the limiting friction.

$0 \leq f \leq \mu N$

$0 \leq f \leq \mu Mg$

Substituting the minimum and maximum values of $f$ into the expression for $F$:

When $f = 0$, $F_{\min} = \sqrt{(Mg)^2 + 0} = Mg$

When $f = \mu Mg$, $F_{\max} = \sqrt{(Mg)^2 + (\mu Mg)^2} = Mg\sqrt{1 + \mu^2}$

Therefore, the total force $F$ exerted by the surface satisfies the condition:

$Mg \leq F \leq Mg\sqrt{1 + \mu^2}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Suppose the contact force exerted by a rough horizontal surface on a body resting on it has a constant magnitude. If the angle between this contact force and the vertical is reduced, the frictional force acting between the body and the surface will
questions.solution:
Let the contact force exerted by the surface on the body be $F_c$.

The contact force can be resolved into two mutually perpendicular components:
1. Normal reaction $N$ acting vertically upwards.
2. Frictional force $f$ acting horizontally along the surface.

Let $\theta$ be the angle between the contact force and the vertical. From the resolution of vectors, the frictional force is given by:
$f = F_c \sin \theta$

It is given that the magnitude of the contact force $F_c$ is constant and the angle $\theta$ is reduced.

For an acute angle $\theta$, as $\theta$ decreases, the value of $\sin \theta$ also decreases.

Therefore, the frictional force $f$ will decrease.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: To prevent slipping when walking on ice, one should take small steps. This is due to the fact that shorter steps ensure
questions.solution:
Let the leg make an angle $\theta$ with the vertical while walking.

The normal force $N$ and the frictional force $f$ provided by the ground are related by $f = N \tan\theta$.

To avoid slipping, the required frictional force must be less than or equal to the limiting friction, i.e., $f \le \mu N$.

Since the coefficient of friction $\mu$ for ice is very small, the required friction $f$ must be small.

Taking shorter steps makes the angle $\theta$ smaller, which in turn reduces the required frictional force $f$.

Therefore, shorter steps ensure smaller friction is needed to walk without slipping.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Observe the situation depicted in the figure. The wall is smooth, whereas the contact surfaces between $A$ and $B$ are rough. In equilibrium, the friction exerted on $B$ by $A$
options: the system cannot remain in equilibrium
questions.solution:
Consider the system comprising both blocks A and B.

The external forces acting on this system in the vertical direction are their respective weights, $m_A g$ and $m_B g$, which act downwards.

Since the wall is smooth, it cannot exert any vertical frictional force on block A. It only provides a horizontal normal reaction.

The applied force $F$ is purely horizontal and provides no vertical component.

Therefore, there is no external upward force to balance the total downward gravitational force $(m_A + m_B)g$.

Since the net vertical force on the system is non-zero, the system cannot remain in equilibrium and will accelerate downwards.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Starting from rest, a scooter travels with a constant acceleration for a time $\Delta t_1$, subsequently moves with a constant velocity for the next $\Delta t_2$, and finally undergoes a constant deceleration for the next $\Delta t_3$ to come to rest. A $500 \text{ N}$ man sitting on the scooter behind the driver manages to remain at rest with respect to the scooter without touching any other part. The force exerted by the seat on the man is
options: less than $500 \text{ N}$ throughout the journey
$> 500 \text{ N}$ for time $\Delta t_1$ and $\Delta t_3$ and $500 \text{ N}$ for $\Delta t_2$
more than $500 \text{ N}$ throughout the journey
questions.solution:
During the time interval $\Delta t_1$, the scooter accelerates with an acceleration $a_1$. The forces acting on the man are his weight $W = 500 \text{ N}$ acting downwards and the force from the seat. Since the man is at rest relative to the scooter, he accelerates with $a_1$. The force exerted by the seat has a vertical normal component $N = 500 \text{ N}$ to balance gravity and a horizontal frictional component $f = m a_1$ to provide the acceleration.

The total force exerted by the seat is $F_1 = \sqrt{N^2 + f^2} = \sqrt{500^2 + (m a_1)^2}$, which is greater than $500 \text{ N}$.

During the time interval $\Delta t_2$, the scooter moves with a constant velocity, so the acceleration is zero. The horizontal force is zero, and the force exerted by the seat is only the vertical normal force $N = 500 \text{ N}$.

During the time interval $\Delta t_3$, the scooter decelerates with an acceleration $a_3$. The force exerted by the seat again has a horizontal component $f' = m a_3$ and a vertical component $N = 500 \text{ N}$. The total force is $F_3 = \sqrt{500^2 + (m a_3)^2}$, which is greater than $500 \text{ N}$.

Thus, the force exerted by the seat on the man is $> 500 \text{ N}$ for time $\Delta t_1$ and $\Delta t_3$, and $500 \text{ N}$ for $\Delta t_2$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Consider the situation shown in the figure. Suppose all the surfaces are rough. The direction of friction on $B$ due to $A$
options: depends on the masses of $A$ and $B$
questions.solution:
Let us consider the forces acting on block $B$ in the vertical direction.

Since the entire system is in equilibrium, block $B$ is also in equilibrium. This means the net vertical force on block $B$ must be zero.

The gravitational force acting on block $B$ is its weight ($m_B g$), which pulls it downwards.

To balance this downward force, there must be an equal upward force. The only surface in contact with $B$ that can provide this vertical force is the surface of block $A$. Thus, the static frictional force exerted by $A$ on $B$ must act in the upward direction.

Therefore, the friction on $B$ due to $A$ is upward.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: To bring a car to a halt over the shortest distance on a horizontal road, one must
options: pump the brakes by alternately pressing and releasing them
apply the brakes extremely hard so the wheels cease rotating
turn off the engine without using the brakes
apply the brakes firmly enough to just avoid slipping
questions.solution:
To stop the car over the shortest possible distance, the deceleration must be maximum.

The deceleration is provided by the frictional force between the tires and the road.

The maximum possible frictional force is the limiting static friction, which is greater than the kinetic friction ($\mu_s > \mu_k$).

If the brakes are applied extremely hard, the wheels lock and slide, resulting in kinetic friction which provides less deceleration.

To utilize the maximum frictional force, the brakes should be applied firmly enough to just avoid slipping.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A boy of mass $M$ exerts a horizontal force to slide a box of mass $M'$ across a rough horizontal surface. The friction coefficient between the boy's shoes and the floor is $\mu$, while that between the box and the floor is $\mu'$. Under which of the following conditions is it definitively impossible to slide the box?
questions.solution:
The maximum horizontal force the boy can exert without slipping backward is given by the limiting friction between his shoes and the floor, $F_{max} = \mu M g$.

The minimum horizontal force required to slide the box is the limiting friction between the box and the floor, $f_{max} = \mu' M' g$.

For the boy to slide the box, he must be able to apply a force greater than the friction on the box without slipping himself. The condition to slide the box is $F_{max} > f_{max}$, which gives $\mu M g > \mu' M' g$, or $\mu M > \mu' M'$.

It is definitively impossible to slide the box if the maximum force the boy can exert is less than the force required to slide the box, which means $\mu M < \mu' M'$.

Analyzing the given options, if $\mu < \mu'$ and $M < M'$, then multiplying these two inequalities for positive quantities yields $\mu M < \mu' M'$. Under this condition, the boy can never exert enough force to slide the box.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two cars possessing unequal masses are equipped with similar tyres. If they travel at identical initial speeds, the minimum stopping distance
options: is shorter for the heavier car
depends on the volume of the car
is shorter for the lighter car
questions.solution:
Let the mass of the car be $m$ and its initial speed be $u$.

The maximum frictional force acting on the car when brakes are applied is $f = \mu N = \mu m g$, where $\mu$ is the coefficient of friction between the tyres and the road.

The deceleration of the car is $a = \dfrac{f}{m} = \dfrac{\mu m g}{m} = \mu g$.

Using the third equation of motion, $v^2 = u^2 - 2as$. For the minimum stopping distance $s$, the final velocity $v = 0$.

$0 = u^2 - 2(\mu g)s$

$s = \dfrac{u^2}{2\mu g}$

Since both cars have similar tyres, the coefficient of friction $\mu$ is the same. They also have identical initial speeds $u$.

The stopping distance $s$ is independent of the mass $m$ of the car. Thus, it is identical for both cars.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A block $A$ placed on an inclined surface just starts to slide when the inclination reaches $30^\circ$. If block $A$ is replaced by another block $B$, it is observed that it just starts to slide when the inclination is $40^\circ$.
options: All three of the above are possible
questions.solution:
The angle of repose $\theta$ is the minimum angle of inclination of a plane at which a block placed on it just begins to slide.

The condition for the block to just start sliding is $mg \sin \theta = \mu_s mg \cos \theta$, which simplifies to $\tan \theta = \mu_s$, where $\mu_s$ is the coefficient of static friction between the block and the inclined surface.

The angle of repose depends only on the nature of the surfaces in contact (coefficient of friction) and is completely independent of the mass of the block.

Since blocks $A$ and $B$ have different angles of repose ($30^\circ$ and $40^\circ$ respectively), their coefficients of friction with the inclined surface are different. This difference provides no information about their masses.

Therefore, the mass of block $A$ can be less than, greater than, or equal to the mass of block $B$. All three cases are possible.
